comments:
Summary:
This paper addresses an important limitation of factorization machines by presenting the first efficient training algorithms for arbitrary-order Higher-Order Factorization Machines (HOFMs). The authors develop dynamic programming (DP) algorithms to evaluate the ANOVA kernel and its gradient in O(d m) time, derive stochastic gradient and coordinate-descent solvers that use these primitives, and introduce two shared-parameter kernels (inhomogeneous ANOVA via an augmented-data trick, and the all-subsets kernel) to reduce model size and prediction cost. Experiments on several link-prediction and recommender datasets show improved performance over relevant baselines and practical solver comparisons.

Strengths:
- Novel and useful algorithmic contribution: the DP evaluation and reverse-mode differentiation of the ANOVA kernel at arbitrary order with linear-time complexity is a clear advance; it enables practical use of HOFMs beyond second order.
- Practical solver development: providing both SG and CD algorithms, with careful attention to sparse data, memory, and computational trade-offs.
- Thoughtful model variants: the shared-parameter kernels and the simple augmented-data trick are elegant and practically useful to reduce model size and inference cost.
- Empirical evaluation: multiple real datasets, comparisons to reasonable baselines (including low-rank bilinear regression and polynomial networks), and solver comparisons (AdaGrad, L-BFGS, CD) that illuminate when each method is preferable.
- Clear complexity analyses and discussion of sparse vs dense settings.

Weaknesses and suggestions for improvement:
- Experimental scope: evaluation is limited to link prediction and a couple recommender settings. It would strengthen empirical claims to include more diverse tasks (classification/regression on standard tabular datasets with high-dimensional sparse features) or larger-scale industrial-style datasets to demonstrate scalability.
- Baselines and ablations: the paper could include stronger or additional baselines (e.g., modern deep models or other higher-order interaction methods) and more ablation studies on hyperparameters (effect of rank k, sensitivity to m, and the impact of the augmented dummy-feature approach vs directly learned θ across multiple settings).
- Optimization details: AdaGrad was sensitive to learning-rate choice; more investigation (or use of alternative adaptive optimizers) could clarify practical recommendations. Some discussion of convergence guarantees for the alternating minimization (when optimizing separate P(t)) would be useful, even if only heuristic.
- Imbalanced data and negative sampling: the experimental protocol samples balanced negatives for AUC evaluation. For many real link-prediction problems negatives are overwhelming; results under highly imbalanced settings or with AUC-maximizing objectives (as mentioned in conclusion) would be informative.
- Clarity and reproducibility: overall exposition is good, but some algorithm pseudocode has indexing that could be tightened for clarity. Numerical issues (e.g., using many dummy features, potential scaling/regularization interactions) are not discussed; releasing code would aid reproducibility.

Minor points:
- The quadratic dependency of the CD algorithm on m limits its practicality for larger orders; the authors note this but concrete strategies (e.g., approximations) could be suggested.
- It would be helpful to report wall-clock training/inference times and memory usage for larger m and k to complement the algorithmic complexity claims.

Overall assessment:
This paper makes a substantial and original contribution by making higher-order FMs practical through solid algorithmic innovations and useful model variants. The empirical results are promising and the paper includes valuable practical insights. I recommend acceptance and dissemination; follow-up work should expand empirical evaluations and address some of the optimization and scalability concerns noted above.
